title: iPhone 17 vs iPhone 17 Pro Max - Qué iPhone comprar?
transcript: Five important differences between the regular iPhone 17 and the iPhone 17 Pro Max. First of all, the 17 gets quite hot when gaming, while we in the iPhone 17 Pro Max have an aluminum unibody chassis along with a vapor chamber, which allows the heat to dissipate evenly throughout the iPhone, resulting in excellent sustained performance and much better gaming.  However, in favor of the 17 we have a 120Hz screen this year, which allows us to be much smoother when moving through the interface.  Now, battery life is the only option this year to have excellent battery levels, and I'm consistently getting around 8 hours of screen time .  Next up are the cameras because these lenses, the main and the ultra-wide-angle, are basically the same.  In our ultragrular we have macro mode where we can get quite close to any object.  The performance of the main sensor is also very similar.  I don't think anyone would notice the difference, but this year in the 17 Pro Max we have a periscope that reaches 4X optical zoom and even up to 40x digitally, and the difference in zoom is quite noticeable, where obviously this has much better quality.  The important difference is in the video because we in the Pro Max can record in a much heavier codec.   It's very noticeable when we directly compare the 4K 30 frames per second on the 17 with the Apple Pro reslock mode that records on the iPhone 17 Pro Max.  What do you think?  Are all these differences worth $500?

title: iPhone 17 vs. Air vs. 17 Pro vs. 17 Pro Max Battery Test!
transcript: After running multiple trials to make sure that we got it right, the iPhone 17 series battery test is finally here, where this year Apple made some big claims when it comes to battery life, saying that the 17 Pro and 17 Pro Max can each last 6 hours longer than last year's models, while the base iPhone 17 actually gets an even bigger jump with Apple saying that it lasts 8 hours longer. The iPhone 17 Pro actually performed the best here, still reporting 100% while the other iPhones show either a one or twopoint drop. Now, those battery indicators aren't necessarily the most accurate early on. So, we'll see what happens here in messaging where all four phones are within equal distance of the mini cell tower in our lab, where this time, after an hour of texting back and forth with our automated chatbots, the iPhone 17 Pro Max does the best, dropping by only four points, while the Air does twice as bad, suffering from an eightoint drop for the same exact task, which immediately puts it behind the other three by a decent bit. And things could get even worse for it here in email where we have each phone opening and scrolling through the same set of threads where 1 hour later that doesn't happen. The Air is able to match the regular 17 with a fivepoint drop while the Pro models each do slightly better dropping by only four points. But we'll see how the Air stacks up here in the browser test. Since unlike the other tasks we've done, this one's a lot more processor intensive. Since each phone is randomly cycling through the same set of websites, they're loading each page while also scrolling through them. Where one hour later, wow, the Air suffers a 14 point drop, which now puts it 10 points behind the Pro models and five points behind even the regular iPhone 17. Now, speaking of the iPhone 17, there are two things that make it different this year. For one, just like the rest of the lineup, it is finally using a 120 Hz prootion display, meaning it'll actually ramp its refresh rate both up and down as we scroll through the home feed. And then two, unlike the rest of the lineup, it's the only one here that doesn't get the A19 Pro chip with it only getting the standard A19. So, it's going to be interesting to see how that plays out through this battery test where at least after an hour in Instagram, it seems to be holding up just fine, matching the iPhone 17 Pro's fivepoint drop and even pulling another point ahead of the iPhone Air. But we'll see what happens here in standby where I'm curious to see how the Air does compared to the other three since it is the only one using Apple's in-house C1X modem while the other three are using Qualcomm's modem. And after 16 hours of just sitting there on idle, it looks like it did significantly better. Not in terms of like actual raw percentage drain, but in terms of its estimated milliamp hour usage. Here in YouTube, it's worth noting that each phone's display has been calibrated to the same 200 nits of brightness with us also matching the speakers to the nearest decel count, which is especially important considering that the iPhone Air only has a single speaker to power compared to the stereo speakers on the other three. And after an hour, it seems to have helped with it technically being the most efficient, at least in terms of milliamp hour usage. Of course, in terms of percentage drain, though, the 17 Pro Max did the best with its 5,000 mAh battery outperforming all the phones, pulling ahead of the smaller 17 Pro as we start working on gaming. Now, while Alto's Adventure isn't the most intensive game out there, it is still taxing the chipsets on each phone, where we'll get to see how the A19 in the regular iPhone 17 compares to the A19 Pro chip in the rest of the phones, where after an hour of gaming non-stop, oh man, the 17 and its standard A19 chip takes a major hit, dropping by 15 points compared to the 10point drop on the iPhone Air, placing the 17 in last place now as the Air slots itself into third. So, not the best performance from the 17 in that gaming test, but we'll see if we can turn things around here in maps where we're simulating navigation with GPS and the compass turned on. title: Weak Apple iPhone 17 Air Sales Forces Apple to Cut Production & Increase iPhone 17 Pro & Pro Max
transcript: When Steve Jobs introduced the original iPhone, it felt like a revelation. Since then, only a couple of models have given us that same wow feeling. The iPhone 10 and now the iPhone Air. This phone is without question a wonderful feeling phone. It is thin, light, and effortless to grip. Watching videos in bed or slipping it into a pocket is a genuinely different experience compared to the thick, heavy phones that dominated the market for years. After being available for about a month, we are starting to hear about struggling sales numbers and Apple going so far as to even cutting production. So, how could this be when all we heard was glorious reviews when it was first launched? Two new market surveys were recently released that sheds some more light on why sales are sluggish. The Key Bank Capital market survey was released earlier today and found virtually no demand for iPhone Air and limited willingness to pay for a foldable. The survey also found healthy but measured demand for the rest of the iPhone 17 lineup with the sales mix continuing to shift towards the iPhone 17 Pro and iPhone 17 Pro Max models. Key Bank Capital is now reporting that Apple is curtailing the production cadence of the iPhone Air while boosting the production orders for the other models in the iPhone 17 lineup. The publication did not give any indication as to the magnitude of these cuts. According to a recent CNET survey of over 2,000 smartphone users, the top reasons people upgrade are price, battery life, storage, camera features, and screen size. The least important reasons were AI features, phone color, phone thinness, and switching ecosystems. In short, thinness, the Air's headline feature appeals to only a small slice of buyers. >> Seeing iPhone 17 sales higher by 14% in the first 10 days than iPhone 16 sales. And not just in the US, but also China. What do we miss, Matt? I thought we weren't really expecting the 17 to be so sellable. >> Yeah, it's interesting. I mean, I think probably a lot of people were slightly underwhelmed with the 16 last year and just decided to hold on uh to see what the next upgrade would bring. I mean, certainly what we've seen today from this counterpoint research is consistent with our own survey work at Blueberg Intelligence where we've definitely seen strong uh demand or interest in the iPhone 17 and um a higher number of people looking to upgrade within the next 3 months. uh but actually when you look at the kind of four to six month view it's uh similar about 30% of customers uh looking to say they're going to upgrade uh so I think it's a com it's always a combination of features it's kind of storage battery life screen uh the camera these kind of things what kind of really spur people on uh and certainly our own survey research kind of is consistent what's heard from counterpoint today uh that there's interest both in the iPhone 17 the base level but also very strong interest in the the pro and the max models the most expensive models In our research, um about 46% of Apple customers planning to upgrade uh said that they were going to go for those expensive models. Only 5% said they were going to go for the new iPhone Air. So perhaps um that product's got some way to go before it kind of really convinces consumers it's got a place in the line. The business publication claims to have learned of a major cut to iPhone air production, motivated by weaker than expected consumer interest, nearly the end of production levels. Despite early reports of the iPhone Air selling out within hours of launch in China, the overall reception has apparently been lukewarm in the region. Combined with weak demand in other markets, sales projections have dropped significantly. Although the publication declined to disclose the full extent of the production cut or which suppliers will be affected, it says that the changes may be seen in Apple's supply chain in the coming months. Last week, Japan's Mizuo Securities said that Apple will reduce iPhone Air production by 1 million units this year. Meanwhile, Apple apparently plans to increase production of iPhone 17 models by 2 million units. A separate report claimed that Samsung has canled plans to release a successor to its own iPhone air rival, the Galaxy S26 Edge, due to low sales. I myself was interested in this device when I first learned about it, but that's before we knew it was only going to have one camera and one speaker and a super small battery and no vapor chamber. It sounds cool at first, but as more and more information was released about the device, the less and less I desired one, I knew this was going to be a disaster. And what the hell was Apple thinking putting this out for consumers is beyond me. Stay tuned and subscribe to the channel. We got information on Apple's new foldable device being delayed. Making the video now. Peace.

title: We Refurbished an iPhone 17 Pro Screen – Harder than 16 Pro ？
transcript: During our iPhone 17 Pro teardown, we not only tested the scratch resistance of the frame but also the screen. The Ceramic Shield 2 shows impressive resistance only the electric grinding pen left deep scratches And replacing such a screen officially would cost $329 At REWA Lab, we take a different approach — front glass repair We first test the screen for its display and touch functions Then, we remove the screen. Normally, the UV laser machine should work on a detached screen But this time, we're testing it on the screen assembled on the iPhone Before starting, we remove the front sensor assembly And cover the front cameras with Kapton tape for protection We are using a fixture to keep the iPhone flat during laser processing Since diagrams for the iPhone 17 series are not available yet we use iPhone 16 Pro's diagram for the laser process The machine uses a UV cold light source that precisely softens the OCA without harming the display The OCA layer develops a slight haze Remove the screen The back also shows some haze, meaning the frame adhesive has softened Place the screen on a separation machine for suction Apply some alcohol around the edges of the frame Start from the camera area, using pliers to grip the frame Gently lift it upward, adding more alcohol to loosen the bond Continue until the entire frame is removed Use tweezers to scrape off the remaining glue ensuring the surface is clean and smooth After the UV laser softens the adhesive, the frame can be removed more easily After removing the frame, we find that the flex cable structure is basically the same as the iPhone 16 Pro We apply some alcohol along the edge of the screen Slide a thin film between the front glass and display from the corner Gently slide it through the gap When it feels stuck, apply more alcohol to soften the adhesive and continue Now, we try to grasp and peel off the display Peeling off seems to be difficult though After repeated attempts, we decided to pause and checked the reason It appears the OCA used in the iPhone 17 Pro's screen is different from the 16 Pro's We will test the UV laser process on an iPhone 16 Pro later to verify For now, to be safe, we switch to the cutting wire Carefully slide the wire between the glass and the display Since the display has already been partially peeled it's much easier for the wire to slide in. then guide it back and forth in a steady motion Continue until the glass is completely detached The display seemed fine — we thought the separation didn't cause any damage The prior UV laser treatment does make the separation process safer Now, we perform the same UV laser process on the iPhone 16 Pro's screen to compare the separation process Remove the frame As we can see, the display and front glass of the 16 Pro separate easily without the need for the cutting wire This confirms that the iPhone 17 Pro's front glass OCA has changed Let's continue with the iPhone 17 Pro We use an electric rotary tool to roll up the remaining OCA Keep the tool as parallel to the display as possible Move the rotary tool slowly in the same direction as its rotation Continue rolling across the entire display surface until large pieces of OCA are removed Bring the display in front of a dust cleaning device Use a sponge dipped in n-Hexane Wipe the display surface with the sponge Repeat the process until most of the residue is removed then switch to a lint-free cloth for the final cleaning. Then we install the new front glass Switch on the green light to make dust particles more visible Keeping the display completely dust-free is crucial Since alignment molds for the iPhone 17 series are not available yet we align the new glass manually Align the Dynamic Island cutout and slightly adjust the display edges to match the front glass then slowly press the display into place Put the screen into a laminating machine to laminate and remove bubbles The front glass is perfectly laminated onto the display, with no air bubbles. At present, no aftermarket screen frames are available for the iPhone 17 series So, for this test, we skip the frame installation step During the final test, we found a black spot near the Dynamic Island The screen shows display damage — refurbishment failed So, what went wrong? After laser separation, the display worked fine But after removing the front glass, damage appeared The issue occurred when we used the thin film for separation Because the new OCA is stickier, the film couldn't slide through smoothly It bent under uneven force and scratched the display, causing damage Since the same UV laser method worked fine on the iPhone 16 Pro, we assumed it's due to the new OCA in the iPhone 17 Pro We tested another iPhone 17 Pro screen using the same steps This time, we only use the cutting wire for separation After separation, everything worked perfectly No damage — the display panel remained intact The new OCA does make refurbishment more challenging We'll report the test results to the REFOX team The UV laser parameters will be optimized for the iPhone 17 Pro and its new OCA

title: IPHONE 17 PRO MAX: NOVO ACABAMENTO e VISUAL ATUALIZADO em mesma proposta  | Análise / Review
transcript: The iPhone 17 Pro Max marks another step in Apple's evolution in the premium segment, with subtle design changes, a new chip, and camera adjustments. The goal is to refine what already worked well, maintaining a focus on performance, image quality, and integration with the ecosystem.  But faced with increasingly balanced competition, can the 17 Pro Max still justify its high price and remain a benchmark among top-of-the-line models?  Check out the full analysis from tudocom. The iPhone 17 Pro Max marks the return of aluminum to the Pro line, abandoning titanium and steel.  The choice prioritizes practicality and lightness, but also reduces the feeling of exclusivity.  The new body features a mix of materials: aluminum, glass, and ceramic, which departs from Apple's traditionally uniform look.  The rear adopts a controversial design with a large camera module that extends across the entire width and a piece of glass over the wireless charging coil.  The transitions between the materials are not entirely smooth, and there are small visible gaps between the glass and the metal, compromising the feeling of industrial precision.  The use of aluminum raises questions about its durability.  The material is softer and more susceptible to scratches and dents from falls, which can even affect its waterproofing.  Nevertheless, the device retains enhanced IP68 certification, supporting immersion up to 6 meters for 30 minutes.  On the front, the Ceramic Shield 2 offers enhanced protection and excellent readability in bright light, with thin bezels and a pill-shaped cutout housing the front camera and Face ID.  The aluminum body features subtle curves that improve ergonomics, while the weight of 230g keeps the device robust. Available in cosmic orange, intense blue, and silver, the model features solid colors and matte surfaces. Orange is the color that draws the most attention, while blue maintains a more classic feel.  The silver, in turn, displays a clear contrast between glass and metal, further highlighting the slight unevenness in the construction.  In terms of connectivity, the iPhone 17 Pro Max offers support for 5G, Wi-Fi 7, Bluetooth 6.0, NFC, and SIM.  Wireless charging remains limited to 25W with MAGSE-compatible accessories. Apple's new flagship phone features a 6.9-inch Super Retina XDR OLED display with Promotion technology, which dynamically adjusts the refresh rate between 1 and 120 Hz. The 1320 x 2868 pixel resolution maintains a density of 460 ppi, ensuring excellent sharpness and fluidity, especially in games and fast scrolling.  The panel retains the pill-shaped cutout, but the main improvement is the new Ceramic Shield Glass 2 protection, now with an anti-reflective coating.  Readability in direct sunlight has noticeably improved, with reduced glare and high contrast maintained even in extremely bright environments.  The screen offers HDR10 Dob Vision and True Tone support, adjusting the color temperature according to the ambient lighting.  HDR video playback impresses with its flexibility.  iOS applies the effect only to the area being played, without requiring a full screen, which enhances immersion and saves energy.  In our measurements, the panel reached approximately 10,000 nits of brightness in manual mode and up to 1,800 nits in automatic mode, exceeding the official specifications.  In outdoor mode, Apple promises brightness of up to 3,000 unnits, making the content readable in any condition.  The automatic refresh rate control is efficient, adapting well between static use and fast animations.  The 17 Pro Max retains the hybrid stereo system with the bottom speaker and call output acting as a second channel.  The result is a balanced and full-bodied sound, with good spatiality.  Although it has lost a slight amount of maximum volume, the difference may be imperceptible in daily use.  The sound quality remains very good, with well-balanced bass and treble .  The voices could use a little reinforcement, but the overall performance still stands out for its clarity and fidelity.  The Tactile engine also deserves a mention.  He remains a benchmark, offering precise and pleasant responses in every interaction.  The iPhone 17 Pro Max comes equipped with the new Apple A19 Pro chip, manufactured using a 3-nanometer process.  It features a six- core CPU, with two high-performance cores at 4.26 GHz and four efficiency cores at 2.6 GHz, accompanied by a six-core GPU with hardware raying support.  The system is aided by a 16-core neural engine dedicated to artificial intelligence tasks, resulting in greater speed for image processing, language, and automation capabilities.  Apple also implemented a new vapor chamber which, combined with the aluminum body, improves heat dissipation and maintains performance for longer.  In stress tests, the new cooling system demonstrated superior efficiency, preventing sudden drops in performance, even after long sessions of intense use.  According to company data, sustained performance gains can reach up to 40% compared to the previous generation, which translates to greater stability in benchmarks and games.  Now with 12 GB of RAM, the new Apple phone offers excellent multitasking management, keeping all applications open in the background.  In our speed test, it outperformed Android rivals and previous generation iPhones, but not enough to dethrone the iPhone 12 Pro Max from the top of our ranking.  In benchmarks, it surpassed 25.5 million points on AnTo, almost 1 million more than its predecessor.  In games, the performance is exemplary.  Titles like PUBG Mobile ran in Ultra HDR and ultra frame rates , while COD Mobile maintained maximum graphics and FPS with complete stability.  The heating remains under control and the system ensures consistent fluidity, even with all graphics features enabled.  The 17 Pro Max received a slight battery upgrade, now with a capacity of 4823 mAh in versions with a physical SIM slot and 88 mAh in variants with ISI. Although the increase is slight, it reinforces the pursuit of greater autonomy without compromising the device's slim design. In our standardized tests, which simulate real-world use with popular apps, social media, video and music streaming, as well as occasional gaming sessions, Apple's top-of-the-line model achieved 31 hours of moderate use.  It's a solid result, but slightly inferior to that of the 16 Pro Max, suggesting that the efficiency gains from the new chip and software haven't yet translated into a practical advantage.  Charging has also evolved with support for the new dynamic charger of up to 60W, sold separately, capable of recovering 50% of the charge in approximately 20 minutes.  Using a conventional adapter, we obtained 37% in 15 minutes and 65% in half an hour, with a full charge being achieved in about 1 hour and 28 minutes. The iPhone 17 Pro Max comes with iOS 26 out of the box, a version that marks an important change in the system's naming convention, now aligned with Apple's product fiscal year.  The jump from iOS 18 to 26 also reflects a complete visual overhaul with the new Liquid Glass identity inspired by Vision OS.  The new design features translucent elements and fluid effects that react dynamically to touch, making navigation more organic.  The interface is more customizable, allowing you to change fonts, colors, icons, and animations.  Native applications, such as photos, cameras, messages, phone, and maps, have been redesigned to follow the new visual standard.  The lock screen received special attention with resizable widgets and wallpapers with 3D depth.  Features such as Always On Display and Standby Mode remain available, displaying essential information while charging.  The system maintains the logic of application pages and libraries, with the freedom to hide specific screens depending on the context of use.  The control center has gained multiple pages and new expandable shortcuts, while the notification panel remains integrated into the lock screen.  The island remains a multitasking hub, displaying alerts, a timer, calls, and music in real time.  Multitasking, while visually unchanged, remains fluid and reliable.  Among the updated apps, the Camera app now centralizes all quality options in a redesigned and more intuitive interface.  The Photos app now has a collections tab and can convert 2D images into three-dimensional scenes.  Safari, Phone, and Messages have gained new features such as call filtering, automatic replies, and AI-powered background generation .  iOS 26 also expands artificial intelligence capabilities with Apple Intelligence, integrating live translation, visual search, and native GPT chat support.  The assistant recognizes elements on the screen, translates conversations in real time, and suggests contextual actions.  It's a significant leap in automation and context, making the system more proactive and intelligent.  The iPhone 17 Pro Max features an updated camera setup with three 48-megapixel rear sensors and a new 18-megapixel front camera.  The highlight is the redesigned telephoto lens, which now uses a larger sensor and a 100mm tetraprism lens. All cameras offer sensor-shift stabilization and can capture images at 24 or 48 megapixels. which ensures greater consistency across the different modes.  The photos from the main camera maintain Apple's signature style.  With sharp images, balanced colors, and well-controlled exposure.  The sensor continues to deliver excellent dynamic range and detailed textures, although processing still smooths faces for more pleasing portraits.  Overall, the 24-megapixel photos have good depth and natural saturation, resulting in a mature and consistent look.  Ultra Wide remains one of the best on the market, offering good sharpness, efficient distortion correction, and color fidelity in different lighting conditions.  Outdoor shots are vibrant, and even in low-light environments, the camera maintains satisfactory performance.  Despite few changes compared to the previous model, the visual uniformity between the sensors is remarkable.  The new 48-megapixel telephoto lens replaces the old five-times zoom with a four-times zoom, prioritizing better quality in mid-range frequencies.  The photos show a great balance of color and exposure, although the processing is more aggressive on fine details, the 12 megapixel mode offers a more natural look, but the real advantage lies in the improved versatility and optical stability.  For close-up photos, Apple retains the macro mode based on the ultra- wide lens, but the 2x zoom of the main camera continues to deliver the best results.  It avoids unwanted distortions and shadows.  capturing more realistic close-ups with good texture.  The macro mode still lacks precision and tends to soften the focus at short distances.  At night, Apple's most advanced phone demonstrates subtle improvements in noise control and exposure balance.  The automatic mode rarely activates night mode, but even so, the photos maintain high definition and accurate colors. Bright spots exhibit a slight burst of light, but the dynamic range remains wide and the processing preserves naturalness.  The 18-megapixel front camera is unprecedented and features an octagonal sensor with a multi-aspect design.  It offers excellent sharpness and effective autofocus, allowing for close-up selfies without loss of quality. The skin tone tends towards warm, especially under artificial lighting, but the overall balance and detail are convincing, even in portrait mode.  In video recording, the iPhone 17 Pro Max continues to be the benchmark in the segment.  It shoots video in up to 4K 120 fps with Do Vision, Proess, and Apple Log 2, maintaining active stabilization in all modes.  The definition is exemplary at all distances, with balanced colors, precise contrast, and ample light dynamics .  Even in motion, the stabilization compensates for vibrations in a natural and fluid way.  In low light, performance remains high, with controlled noise and excellent color reproduction.  There are still internal reflections in scenes with intense light sources, but the overall result remains above average.  The captured spatial audio offers realistic immersion and crystal-clear quality, reinforcing the iPhone 17 Pro Max's status as one of the best for video today.  iPhone 17 Pro Max or Galaxy S25 Ultra?  Which is the best?  The two share similar dimensions and screen quality, differing only in the more curved design at the corners on the Apple model.  In terms of water resistance, the iPhone has the advantage, while the Galaxy stands out for offering superior brightness, reduced reflections, and more powerful audio.  In terms of pure performance, Apple maintains its lead, but Samsung wins in battery life and charging speed.  Both deliver excellent results in photos and videos, with the iPhone retaining the crown in video recording.  The Realme GT7 Pro emerges as a worthy competitor, offering IP69 certification for enhanced protection, brightness equivalent to that of an iPhone, and excellent audio quality.  The 17 Pro Max is still superior in fluidity and multitasking performance, but the Realme device offers a much longer-lasting battery and recharges in half the time.  Despite the good balance of cameras, the Apple model still maintains an advantage in consistency, dynamic range, and professional recording.  The iPhone 17 Pro Max arrived on the Brazilian market in September 2025 in four versions.  The cheapest one with 256 GB of storage starts at R$ 12,500, while the most advanced one with 2 TB costs R$ 18,500.   Is it worth paying all that for a cell phone? Apple's new flagship phone maintains the expected quality standard of a branded phone, with stable performance, fluid software, and cameras that remain among the best on the market, especially for videos.  The screen maintains a high standard and the sound is well- defined, but without significant advancements.  Still, range has regressed in this generation, and fast charging still lags behind its rivals.  The new aluminum body also raises questions about its long-term durability.  Ultimately, the iPhone 17 Pro Max is a solid device, but one that focuses more on consistency than real innovation and may seem like a modest upgrade for those already using a recent model from the brand. And to find the best deals for it, you already know, just click on the links below.  Take this opportunity to comment on what you thought of the review of Apple's new flagship phone.  I'll wrap it up here.  A big hug to you and see you next time.

title: Testing a $22 Amazon case on my FAKE iPhone 17 Pro Max!
transcript: Apple's latest iPhone 17 Pro models have a big problem. For the first time since the iPhone X, which kicked off Apple's use of stronger metals like stainless steel and titanium, the new Pro iPhones are made from aluminum. Lighter, yes, better thermals, sure, but also way more prone to dents, dings, and scratches. And if you've been used to just throwing on a thin clear case and not thinking twice about it, like I have with every Pro model for years, this year's phone might surprise you. I put the official Apple Clear Case on my iPhone 17 Pro Max, the same kind I've used for past launches. And after just one normal three-foot drop, there was visible damage in areas the case was supposed to protect. That's never happened before. So, I went on the hunt for something tougher. And that's when I found this, the Subcase Unicorn Beetle Mag. A $21 case that's been all over Amazon, promising serious drop protection, Mag Safe, a built-in kickstand, and even a camera shutter button. Sounds like a lot, but does it actually live up to it? When I first pulled the case out of the box, I didn't expect it to look this good. The deep translucent blue back immediately stood out. And paired with the orange iPhone 17 Pro Max, it looks sharp and intentional, not just functional. The orange action button on the phone even matches the accent color on the case. Still, gray looks can be deceiving, especially with budget cases. Many times they look premium until you hold them, but this one actually felt solid. The rubber edges grip well in the hand. The clear back feels reinforced, and the metal frame around the camera bump gives it a sturdy structure without adding too much weight. Switching to a more protective case usually means dealing with added bulk or slipperiness, which can make daily use a chore. But surprisingly, this case stays comfortable in the hand. The textured sides add just enough grip to keep the phone from shifting or sliding. And the rounded edges help it sit nicely in your palm during one-handed use. Even while walking around, scrolling, or adjusting midstep, the phone always feels secure. It's not a featherweight case, but the balance between protection and comfort is actually better than I expected at this price point. One feature I didn't expect to love as much as I do is the built-in kickstand. On most budget cases, it's either flimsy or missing altogether. This one folds out smoothly from the back and it's made from actual zinc alloy metal. It's sturdy enough for watching YouTube, taking FaceTime calls, or using your phone hands-free while cooking. Protect mode is available, too, though it's slightly less stable than landscape. Still, for a case that costs less than lunch, the fact that this kickstand even exists and works well is a win. Mag Safe compatibility was another question mark going in. A lot of affordable cases promise Mags Safe, but then fall short in actual magnet strength or alignment. That wasn't the case here. With N52 grade magnets rated for up to 1,800 grams of holding force, my iPhone locks securely to Maxive chargers and car mounts. No repositioning, no slow sliding off the pad, no spinning on the mount. It just snaps on and stays put. And visually, the light blue maxive ring blends nicely with the rest of the case. Then there's the camera control button, a feature you almost never see on budget cases. It uses copper conductors built into the case to trigger Apple's camera app and shutter, even without tapping the screen. In theory, it's super convenient, especially for parents, vloggers, or anyone taking quick snapshots, but in real use, it's a little finicky. I noticed the camera launching a few times just from adjusting my grip or picking up the phone off a table. If you tend to carry your phone in your pocket or fidget with it, this button could activate unintentionally. It's a cool bonus, but not something I'd rely on all the time. Now, after what happened to my real iPhone with the Apple Clear Case, I wasn't about to keep risking more expensive damage just to test armor style cases. So, instead, I picked up a dummy iPhone 17 Pro Max off eBay. It has glass on the front and back, a plastic frame, and nearly identical in size and weight to the real thing. While it doesn't have the exact same materials, it's realistic enough to test how cases absorb impact. And it lets me see where damage shows up, both on the case itself and on any exposed parts of the phone. With the subcase, I did six or seven drops from about 2 to 4 feet. Typical couch, desk, and pocket height scenarios. After the first three drops, I noticed parts of the phone's edge starting to crack and break off, especially around one corner. During one of the later drops, even though the phone didn't land on the camera bump, the tempered glass over one of the camera lenses actually popped off, which really caught me off guard. After seeing that second bit of damage to the corner, I decided to stop the test early, mainly because this is the only dummy unit I have, and I still need it for other case reviews and drop comparisons. That said, there was no visible damage to the actual camera housing or the screen. And aside from that one corner, the case itself still looked flawless. The back panel, sides, and kickstand all stayed intact. So, while it's clearly not invincible, this test showed the subcase can take a beating and still protect the most important parts of your phone, especially for something priced just over 20 bucks. As good as it's been, there are a few things you should know. If you use a separate camera lens protector, it likely won't fit. The camera cutout here is tight, and the metal frame sits high. A few reviews mentioned quality control issues, like visible glue around the mag ring or minor sharp edges near the camera, though my unit didn't have those problems. The front lip of the case is raised enough to help prevent screen damage during a flat drop, but it still allows for easy swiping, especially upward gestures from the bottom edge, which taller lips can sometimes interfere with. That said, I personally would prefer something with a slightly higher lip to give the screen more protection overall. If you feel the same, adding a tempered glass screen protector could be a great middle ground, offering that extra layer of defense without changing how the case feels in the hand. So, while this isn't the ultimate solution, it's definitely a step up in protection, especially at just $21.99. title: Fenomena iPhone 17 di China: Borong, Antri, Jual Lagi di Depan Toko!
transcript: In China and Hong Kong, the phenomenon of hoarding iPhone 17s and then selling them again right in front of Apple stores is rampant.  They are called scalpers, namely people who buy up iPhone 17s so that the stock in Apple stores is empty and then resell them at a slightly higher price. Around Rp. 200,000 more expensive for the iPhone 17, Rp. 00,000 for the Pro, and around 2 million more expensive for the iPhone 17 Pro Max.  There are various reasons why people want to buy from scalpers.  There are those who are lazy to queue at official stores.  There are those who are lazy when offered complementary products by sales.  And there are those who only want to buy the iPhone 17 orange variant. Because the stock in the official store has been sold out, they have no choice but to buy it from the scelper for this orange color variant. title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs. Galaxy S25 Ultra: A REAL Day Camera & Battery Test
transcript: ﻿The Galaxy S25 Ultra or iPhone 17 Pro Max? I’m  going to compare the two in a day-in-the-life   during my trip to Albania. Like always,  I charged both phones to 100% and started   my day around 9 in the morning. The iPhone 17  series has an anti-reflective coating this year,   which is nice because it improves the visibility  of the screen. But the coating is not as strong   as the one on the Galaxy S25 Ultra, which reflects  even less light. Tirana is a lovely city and the   capital of Albania, not very far from the coast  of the Adriatic Sea. I put both phones to track my   activity during the day using an app called Komoot  while exploring the city.I took this photo with   the main lens — the iPhone photo is more contrasty  here. On this ultrawide shot, the Galaxy photo is   slightly colder. The main telephoto lens on the  Galaxy is 5x, and on the iPhone it’s 4x, so you   get slightly more range on the Galaxy. Also, the  iPhone can only zoom up to 40x, but the Galaxy   can zoom up to 100x, as I did here on this power  grid that gives me anxiety. For the portrait photo   with the telephoto lens, the photos are similar in  optical quality, but the Galaxy nailed the color   better — my white shirt is actually white and  not blue. We arrived at a nice café called Tony   to have breakfast. The best part of the breakfast  was that a kitty came to greet us. I simply love   stray cats; in Austria, we don’t have any. Both  phones have a similar color profile, and it’s long   gone the time when Galaxy phones oversaturated  images. I also put both phones to play YouTube   in the background while finishing breakfast,  and by the time we were done, the Galaxy had   93% and the iPhone 99%. Both phones were on auto  brightness, and I usually notice the Galaxy dims   the screen a bit more than the iPhone. There was  a marathon going on in the city, and we decided to   walk around the lake in the south and enjoy the  weather. The place we went to is called Tirana   Lake Park, and it was a perfect spot to spend a  day in nature without being far from the city.   I took this photo with the main lens, and it’s  almost impossible to distinguish between the two.   The same goes for the ultrawide shot, where the  only difference is the slight color science — the   leaves are a deeper green on the Galaxy and more  muted on the iPhone. Even on the telephoto lens,   the quality is quite comparable, at least in  daylight. Maybe the only picture that really shows   a different optical quality is this macro shot,  where the Galaxy simply did a better job nailing   the contrast. Generally, these days I would  argue the difference in photos is negligible,   but video is a different story. The Galaxy has  a visibly more vibrant tone on all four lenses.   Also, the Galaxy has a 3x lens that the iPhone  lacks, so you get better optical quality at 3x   zoom compared to the iPhone. The iPhone’s colors  are more muted, which you may actually prefer.   I went back to the hotel room, which was close  to the park, and at this point the Galaxy had   87% while the iPhone had 95% battery left. I then  took a taxi to go to the airport, and I definitely   didn’t spend enough time in Tirana. Write down in  the comments if you’re from Albania or any Balkan   country — I’d love to know. The airport was small  but charming, with a nice panorama view over the   mountains. So I decided to take some photos and  videos. In this ultrawide video, we once again see   that the Galaxy is exposing the scene a bit more  while also making the video more vivid. I also   ordered one of my favorite snacks, cheese burek.  Before I boarded the airplane, the Galaxy had 77%   and the iPhone had 87% battery left. For the first  time in my entire life, I was the first person   to board the plane and was able to take a video  of other passengers. I shot this video with the   Galaxy because, with the Pro Video mode, you can  manually do a focus pull — meaning you can shift   focus from the foreground to the background  or vice versa to create a cinematic effect.   The camera app on the iPhone is rather limited  for such things. For stabilization, they both   do a fine job, and I don’t see a large difference  between them, but the Galaxy has a bit more light   shift, which isn’t great. We flew over some of  the most beautiful aerial scenery I’ve ever seen,   and it made me want to come back here with my  bicycle next time. For the zoom pictures, both   phones did a good job — the Galaxy photos have a  warmer tone, while the iPhone’s are colder. I also   feel the iPhone does slightly better processing  when taking pictures through the window, as it   removes reflections more effectively. But as I  mentioned before, at the end of the day, you won’t   notice much difference between the cameras of  the two phones. I took this picture with 3x zoom,   and here the Galaxy delivers a slightly sharper  result because of the second optical lens it has.   We entered Austrian airspace only an hour later,  and I took this picture over Austria — I do think   the Galaxy nailed the colors better here. Another  advantage of the Galaxy is the 200MP main camera.   If you look at this picture, you don’t really see  any advantage in using the 200MP mode. But if you   crop the same picture, you can see how much more  detail the Galaxy produces compared to the iPhone,   simply because it has more pixels. So if you  plan to print the photo, maybe pick the Galaxy.   For video, even though I like the Galaxy’s  colors and there’s nothing wrong with them,   if I had to pick only one, I’d probably go with  the iPhone during daylight. We landed in Austria,   and I took the train back home. At this point, the  Galaxy had 61% and the iPhone 72% battery left. By   the time I got home, the Galaxy had 50% and the  iPhone had 63% battery left. I put both phones   to upload the photos to Google Cloud, which takes  forever — but that’s not the case when I use the   sponsor of today’s video, UGreen. The same file  uploads to the UGREEN NAS DH2300 in a second,   because the speed is up to 125 megabytes per  second, way faster than any cloud service.   The UGREEN NAS is your best backup solution,  your own private cloud with no monthly fees.   It can store up to 60 terabytes of data, that’s  around 20 million photos or 40 thousand movies,   all kept locally and safely. My phone album also  backs up automatically there, and my Mac’s Time   Machine saves everything straight to the NAS.  No cables, and no risk of losing data. You   can also use NFC to connect your phone to the  NAS instantly. I can set different permissions   for multiple accounts, so everyone only sees  what they need. And with multi-client support,   I can access and manage everything from my phone,  PC, or even remotely through the app — anytime,   anywhere. Thanks to RAID redundancy, even if one  drive fails, all my files stay safe on the second   drive. Check the link in the description and use  my code to get 20% OFF, same price as Black Friday   and thanks to UGREEN for sponsoring this video!  Software-wise, I’m running the latest version of   One UI 8 on the Galaxy and the latest version of  iOS 26 on the iPhone. One UI has a more classic   look, while the iPhone — love it or hate it — has  a different design called Liquid Glass. They share   a lot of similar features, though. For example,  if you hold down the power button, you can talk   to Gemini or Siri. There’s also the Circle to  Search feature on both phones. On the iPhone,   you have to take a screenshot and then scratch the  part of the photo you want to search, while on the   Galaxy you can simply hold the bottom of the phone  and circle around the object — I really like this   feature and use it often. The iPhone has one clear  advantage: it works better if you’re in the Apple   ecosystem, like if you use iCloud or AirDrop.  Android, however, has a few distinct advantages   that make me like it more than iOS — even though  I use a MacBook and Mac Studio. For one, it has   proper file management, which I really appreciate  and makes the phone more useful for productivity.   It also has a clipboard manager, which iOS lacks,  allowing you to access the history of images or   text you’ve copied. And most importantly, it has  a universal back gesture, which iOS still doesn’t   have. On iOS, you often have to guess how to  go back — and I kid you not, in some apps,   there’s simply no way, so you end up switching  tabs instead. From a physical point of view,   one clear advantage of the iPhone is that it has  MagSafe. So if you use a wireless magnetic power   bank, it snaps right onto your phone, whereas  with the Galaxy you need a special case. For me,   this isn’t a big issue because I always use a  case anyway — I use Pitaka cases on my phones. If   you’re interested, I’ll link all the accessories I  used in the description. The other thing about the   iPhone is that you get two extra buttons on the  side — one is the Action button, and the other is   the camera button and slider. On the Galaxy, you  get something even better: the S Pen. And I’m one   of those people who actually use the S Pen daily.  I then went outside to take some night photos.   In this photo with the main lens, the Galaxy photo  has more of a green tint, while the iPhone has a   more magenta one. In the second photo, the Galaxy  nailed the colors better, but the iPhone photo   is sharper and more detailed. For selfies, it’s  not even a contest — the iPhone beats the Galaxy   easily. The quality is much better, and not only  is it wider, but the selfie sensor is also square,   so you can take both horizontal and vertical  shots. On the other hand, for zoom photos,   the Galaxy is simply better. No matter how hard  I tried to take a steady shot with the iPhone,   the photos all turned out blurry. For video,  the quality is quite comparable, but the Galaxy   again does a better job with color science, while  the iPhone looks too warm. When I got back home,   the Galaxy had 47% and the iPhone had 61% battery  left. I also did a quick benchmark, and the iPhone   scored better in both single-core and multi-core  tests — which is expected since the Galaxy is nine   months older and not using the latest Snapdragon  chipset yet. To drain the batteries completely,   I connected the phones to each other and talked  via Google Meet. By the end, the Galaxy had 21%   while the iPhone had 39% battery left. As a note, I did use the nano SIM version   of the iPhone, so you’ll get even better  results with the eSIM-only version. Also,   neither of these phones is my personal device, and  each has fewer than 15 charging cycles. The iPhone   17 Pro Max offers better battery life, and its  selfie camera is clearly superior. I also prefer   the video quality of the iPhone, at least during  daylight, when ghost light reflections aren’t an   issue. The iPhone also supports MagSafe and is  the better choice if you already have other Apple   products. The Galaxy, on the other hand, has an  S Pen and a second telephoto camera, which gives   you an advantage in certain specific scenarios.  It’s also generally more feature-rich, with a   better camera app, and some people may prefer  the freedom that Android offers over iOS. Each   battery test will give slightly different results  depending on the testing conditions. If you want   to see the other battery tests I’ve done for these  two phones and other flagship phones of the year,   you can watch this video next. Thanks for tuning  in to Techmo, and I’ll see you in the next one!

title: iPhone 13 Pro vs iPhone 17 Pro: Watch This Before You BUY or UPGRADE
transcript: So, this is the iPhone 13 Pro. This phone in this graphite color was my daily driver for a solid 2 years. It's been an incredible device, and honestly, even now in 2025, it's still an amazing iPhone for most daily casual tasks. And this this is the brand new iPhone 17 Pro. The question I've been asking myself, and probably the reason you're here, is after 4 years, is this the upgrade we've actually been waiting for? In this video, we're going to break that down side by side. This is for anyone still holding on to a 13 Pro or maybe even an 11 or 12 Pro and you're wondering if now is the time to make that big jump. I skipped the 14 Pro, went with the iPhone 15 Pro, and then skipped the 16 Pro because for me, the upgrades felt a bit, you know, incremental. So, today we're looking at a true 4 generation leap. We'll compare everything from the design and display to the performance and battery and of course that huge camera upgrade. Let's get into it. All right, let's start with how these phones look and feel. In my left hand, the iPhone 13 Pro. This design is iconic for its era. A surgical grade stainless steel frame with these sharp flat edges. It has this heft, this dense premium feel that honestly still feels top tier today. The display is a 6.1 in Super Retina XDR screen. And this was the phone that first brought us prootion. That super smooth 120 Hz adaptive refresh rate was a genuine gamecher back in 2021, and it still makes scrolling feel incredibly fluid. Now, let's look at the iPhone 17 Pro. It's a totally different approach. Instead of stainless steel, we have a uni-body aluminum design. And I know what you might be thinking. Aluminum? Isn't that a downgrade? And yeah, when you hold them side by side, the 13 Pro feels denser, but the 17 Pro is just so comfortable. The more rounded edges feel great in the hand, and it's surprisingly light. The screen is also a bit bigger at 6.3 in, but because the bezels are so much thinner, the overall size difference isn't that dramatic. And this display is ridiculously bright, hitting 3,000 nits peak outdoor brightness. But with this new aluminum design, there is something really important to consider. the scratch gate issue. Early reviews and durability tests like from JerryRrig Everything showed that the anodized coating on the aluminum can chip, especially on the sharp corners of this new camera plateau. It seems to be most noticeable on the colored models like cosmic orange and deep blue. That's honestly why I went with this silver model. It should hide any potential nicks a lot better. So, if you're going caseless, that's definitely something to keep in mind. So, how do they perform? Honestly, for most things, the iPhone 13 Pro is still fantastic. The A15 Bionic chip in here is no slouch. I ran Geekbench 6 on it, and it pulled a 2,417 on single core and 5,766 on multi-core. For day-to-day stuff, opening apps, swiping around, multitasking, it's still incredibly smooth. A big reason for that is the 6 GB of RAM, which really helps keep apps from reloading in the background. It even handles the new liquid glass design language in iOS 26 really well, which is pretty resourceheavy with all its new fluid animations. But the iPhone 17 Pro is just an absolute monster. It's got the new A19 Pro chip and my Geekbench 6 scores were just insane. 3,822 for single core and 9,848 for multi-core. For context, that multi-core score is faster than an M2 iPad Pro. It's desktop class power in a phone. It also has 12 GB of RAM, double the 13 Pro, so multitasking is on another level. But here's the real magic, how it handles that power. For the first time, Apple put a vapor chamber cooling system in an iPhone. And you can feel it. When I was setting this phone up, which is usually when iPhones get pretty warm, this stayed noticeably cool. This is a huge deal for sustained performance, especially for gaming. And this is where that move to aluminum makes perfect sense. The vapor chamber pulls heat away from the chip, and the aluminum body acts like a big radiator to get rid of that heat, which it does way better than titanium or steel. Now, for a huge one, the battery. My iPhone 13 Pro's battery health is down to 71%. And at this point, it's it's pretty bad. I mean, if I try to use this as my main phone for a day, I'm charging it two, sometimes three times. The standby drain is also way faster than it used to be. It's just not reliable anymore. And I know I'm not alone here. A quick look at forums shows tons of users saying the same thing. Once the battery health drops below 80%, the daily endurance just falls off a cliff. The A15 chip is still so capable. It makes the failing battery feel even more frustrating. The iPhone 17 Pro, on the other hand, is a massive leap forward. The battery life is incredible, and that's partly thanks to the new unibody design that made room for a larger battery. Paired with the more efficient A19 Pro chip, it's a night and day difference. This is one of the most significant realworld upgrades you'll feel coming from a 13 Pro. Okay, let's talk about the biggest upgrade of all, the camera. This is where the four years really, really show. The iPhone 13 Pro has a fantastic triple 12 megapixel system. You get a 3x optical zoom and the digital zoom goes up to 15x. It also introduced cinematic mode, which was mind-blowing at the time, letting you create that rack focus effect in video. Though it was limited to 1080p for a day-to-day or quick photo for social media, it's still solid. But this is where you feel the age the most. With the iPhone 17 Pro, we're going from a triple 12 megapixel system to a triple 48 megapixel system. The detail from the new larger sensors is just on another level, but it's the zoom that's the real story. On the 17 Pro, because of those 48 megapixel sensors, you get incredible quality at more in 5x, 1x, a 2x crop, a 4x optical zoom, and even an 8x optical quality zoom. It's like having a whole bag of lenses in your pocket. The quality gap is just insane. And you can push the digital zoom all the way to 40x. For video, you can now shoot in 4K at 120 fps for some truly cinematic slow motion. a big step up from 4K at 60 on the 13 Pro. And the front camera is this wild new 18 megapixel center stage camera that can shoot landscape video even when you're holding the phone vertically. It's a fun and genuinely useful upgrade. Now, software. The iPhone 13 Pro runs iOS 26 beautifully, but it does not support Apple intelligence. That requires the A7 Pro chip or newer. So, is that a deal breakaker for me? Honestly, no. And here's why. You know, Apple Intelligence right now is still in its early days. It's kind of like a public beta. And for the heavy lifting AI stuff, I usually find myself using more powerful alternatives anyway, like the Gemini or Chat GPT apps on my phone. They're incredibly capable and available on any modern iPhone. So, while it's a difference for my workflow, it's not the reason I'd upgrade. So, after all that, where do we land? When you look at these two phones side by side, the 4-year gap represents a truly massive leap in almost every single category. With that in mind, here are my final recommendations. First, if you're holding an iPhone 13 Pro right now and your biggest complaints are the battery life and maybe the inconvenience of the lightning port, my advice is simple. Don't upgrade. Spend $100, get a new battery from Apple, and you can easily ride this phone out for another one or two years. The day-to-day performance for most tasks is still excellent. However, if you are a content creator, a serious mobile photographer, a hardcore mobile gamer, or just someone who wants the absolute best and most versatile camera, then yes, the iPhone 17 Pro is the first truly massive meaningful upgrade for you in 4 years. The jump in camera versatility with that 8x zoom and the sustained performance from the new thermal system is not just incremental, it's transformative for you. the upgrade is absolutely worth it. Now, if you're on a budget, a refurbished iPhone 13 Pro for around $300 is one of the best values in tech. Just make sure it has good battery health and you'll have a flagship experience for a mid-range price. But if you're buying new and you want a device that will be at the top of its game for the next 4 to 5 years, the iPhone 17 Pro is without a doubt the one to get. The performance, headroom, and the versatility of that new camera system are built for the future. But that's just my take after digging through all the data. I really want to hear from you. If you're still rocking an iPhone 13 Pro in 2025, what's your battery health at? And what's the one feature in the 17 Pro that tempts you the most? Drop it all in the comments below. Let's talk about it. If you found this video helpful in making your decision, a like and subscribe would be amazing. Thanks so much for watching and I'll see you in the next

title: Le tout nouvel iPhone 17 Pro orange... changerait de couleur
transcript: The brand new orange iPhone 17 Pro is changing color.  According to several posts on Redit and TikTok, some models have turned from bright orange-pink just a few weeks after their release.  The affected areas would mainly be the aluminum edges and the camera module.  Some specialist websites claim that this discoloration could come from a chemical reaction related to oxidation or the use of unsuitable cleaning products .  But for now, Apple has not given any official explanation. The phenomenon remains quite rare for now , but it reignites the debate on the finish of this premium iPhone model, which has already been criticized for its easy scratches. Tekenco contacted Apple, who stated they had no comment on the matter at this time.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs iPhone 14 Pro Max — Worth Upgrading After 3 Years?
